coredata.dc:title: Visible light-excited full-color phosphorescent material realized by carbon dots dispersed in polyacrylamide and applied to anti-counterfeiting
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Huiyong
Shi, Bowen
Yu, Hongmei
Yang, Shu
Nie, Guochao
Wang, Shaoyan
Chen, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots-based phosphorescent composites have excellent room-temperature phosphorescent properties. However, the development of carbon dots-based phosphorescent materials that are excited by visible light and have adjustable colors remains a challenge. Here, we report on phosphorescent composites based on multicolored fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and polyacrylamide (PAM) with four phosphorescent colors (blue, green, yellow and red). The phosphorescence of CDs@PAM composites is mainly derived from the C=O bonds of CDs. Electron transition of n-π* in the C=O bonds facilitates the intersystem crossover that are necessary to generate phosphorescence. In addition, the hydrogen bond actions between the amide groups of PAM polymer and the functional groups on CDs surface also promote the intersystem crossover and inhibit the non-radiative relaxation of triple states (T1) in CDs@PAM. Meanwhile, the PAM matrix provides a rigid protective environment (hydrogen bond mesh structure) that inhibits vibrations, rotations and collisions of the CDs' luminescent clusters (C=O), avoiding the rapid decay of phosphorescence. Four kinds of CDs based phosphorescence materials can produce brilliant phosphorescence under the excitation of visible light (flashlight). Finally, their colorful phosphorescent colors were used to achieve a naked-eye observable anti-counterfeit pattern, demonstrating the potential application of these materials.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: Texas Library Coalition for United Action (TLCUA): Gold OA 2023
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Polyacrylamide
Visible light
Phosphorescent
Hydrogen bond

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a–d) TEM images of four CDs. Inset, the particle size distributions and HRTEM images of four CDs. e) XRD patterns of four CDs. f) FTIR spectra of four CDs. g) XPS spectra of four CDs. h) Raman spectra of four CDs.
a–d) The normalized UV–Vis absorbance, FL excitation, and emission spectra of four CDs in water. e–h) FL emission spectra of four CDs in water.

a-d) Solid fluorescence spectra of four CDs@PAM. e) FT-IR spectra of PAM and CDs@PAM. f) Afterglow emission spectrum of four CDs@PAM under WLED excitation. g) CIE coordinates corresponding to the phosphorescence spectra of the four CDs@PAM.
a) Diagram of process mechanisms. b) Images of the badge of University of Science and Technology Liaoning under daylight, and visible light irradiation on and off.
The FL emission peaks, RTP emission peaks, and calculated ΔEST of four CDs@PAM composites.

head.ce:title: Visible light-excited full-color phosphorescent material realized by carbon dots dispersed in polyacrylamide and applied to anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Agricultural Resource Chemistry and Biotechnology, Yulin, Guangxi 537000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots-based phosphorescent composites have excellent room-temperature phosphorescent properties. However, the development of carbon dots-based phosphorescent materials that are excited by visible light and have adjustable colors remains a challenge. Here, we report on phosphorescent composites based on multicolored fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and polyacrylamide (PAM) with four phosphorescent colors (blue, green, yellow and red). The phosphorescence of CDs@PAM composites is mainly derived from the C=O bonds of CDs. Electron transition of n-π* in the C=O bonds facilitates the intersystem crossover that are necessary to generate phosphorescence. In addition, the hydrogen bond actions between the amide groups of PAM polymer and the functional groups on CDs surface also promote the intersystem crossover and inhibit the non-radiative relaxation of triple states (T1) in CDs@PAM. Meanwhile, the PAM matrix provides a rigid protective environment (hydrogen bond mesh structure) that inhibits vibrations, rotations and collisions of the CDs' luminescent clusters (C=O), avoiding the rapid decay of phosphorescence. Four kinds of CDs based phosphorescence materials can produce brilliant phosphorescence under the excitation of visible light (flashlight). Finally, their colorful phosphorescent colors were used to achieve a naked-eye observable anti-counterfeit pattern, demonstrating the potential application of these materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have received much attention and made great progress in research related to their fluorescence emission properties [1,2], regulation [3], mechanism [4,5] and potential applications [6-8] in recent years. By selecting suitable precursors and preparation methods, it was achieved to cover from the entire visible to near infrared region [9] for CDs of fluorescence emission, and the corresponding mechanisms and applications were proposed. Although much has been achieved in terms of the luminescent properties and applications of CDs, the research into the afterglow phenomenon is still in its infancy. Nevertheless, researchers are still working on the implementation of afterglow and further exploring its potential applications.

Afterglow materials use light to store energy and retain light emission when the excitation source is removed; thus, also known as luminescent pearls [10-13]. Due to their long luminescence lifetime and large Stokes shift, these materials were widely used in information anti-counterfeiting [14-17], information encryption [18,19], bio-imaging [20], optical sensing [21,22], etc. Mechanism wise, the long-lived afterglow of these carbon-based nanomaterials were mainly attributed to the emission of their excited triplet state, i.e. phosphorescence and delayed fluorescence. The reported CDs-based room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials were generally only excited by the UV light, and their excitation spectra in the UV region were not convenient for some applications [23-25]. As compared to UV light, visible light is safer to use, penetrates more deeply into bioanalysis and imaging, making it easy to use in practical applications. Unfortunately, it was a major challenge to develop afterglow materials obtained by visible light excitation.

At present, only a few studies have reported phosphorescence of CDs under visible light excitation. For example, Gao [26] et al. prepared visible light-excited nitrogen-doped yellow RTP-CDs by a one-step solvothermal method using diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid as a single precursor. Cheng [27] et al. obtained visible-light-excited yellow afterglow CDs@BA by dispersing CDs into Boric acid matrix. Mo [28] et al. obtained green afterglow F, NCDs@SiO2 with visible light excitation by confining fluorine and nitrogen co-coupled CDs to silica nanoparticles. However, regardless of how to obtain visible light-excited RTP-CDs, most of the current CDs-based RTPs were still at short wavelengths, and there were less research on long-wavelength RTP CDs, which limited the application of CDs in the field of secure encryption. Long wavelength phosphorescence has the advantages of high resistance to background interference and long signal transmission distances, bringing new colors and forms to the encrypted display of information [29,30]. Therefore, it is urgent to prepare visible-light-excited full-color phosphorescent CDs.

The development of panchromatic CDs-based phosphorescent materials based on visible light excitation is promising but also challenging and difficult without the help of matrixes. In this paper, phosphorescent materials with adjustable phosphorescent wavelengths between 450 and 610 nm have been achieved by using multicolored fluorescent CDs compounded with polyacrylamide (PAM). It was analyzed that the phosphorescence production mainly originates from the C=O bonding of the CDs and is additionally inextricably linked to the hydrogen bonding environment provided by the PAM matrix and the introduction of a large number of amide groups. Finally, based on these wavelength-tunable phosphorescent composites, naked-eye observable anti-counterfeiting patterns were prepared, demonstrating their potential anti-counterfeiting capability.

Glucose, m-phenylenediamine (m-PD), and potassium rhodanide were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Resorcinol, NaOH, NaHCO3, quinine sulfate, absolute ethyl alcohol, ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid, vitriol, and PAM (M.W:300w) were purchased from Sinopharm chemical reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Rhodamine B was purchased from Tianjin Dengke Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Pomegranate powder was grinded from dried pomegranate shell. All other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Water for solution preparation was ultrapure water prepared by Milli-Q ultrapure water system.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of CDs were taken on a JEOL JEM-ARM200F machine (JEOL, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The UV absorption spectra of CDs were scanned on a U-3900 ultraviolet and visible spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of CDs were carried out using a Bruker Hyperion FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) of CDs was analyzed by using an X'Pert Powder X-ray diffractometer made by Panacol in the Netherlands. Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of CDs and CDs@PAM were obtained using an Edinburgh FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, England). Raman spectra of CDs were measured on an inVia confocal Raman microscope (Renishaw, England). X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of CDs were performed on an ESCALAB 250Xi electron energy spectrometer from Thermo, USA. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs was measured with a Fluorolog 3–11 (HORIBA, Japan).

Four fluorescent CDs were prepared by hydrothermal synthesis (Fig. 1 ). The raw material for the preparation of a-CDs was resorcinol (0.3 g) dissolved in 10 mL of aqueous NaOH (1 M); the raw material for the preparation of b-CDs was m-PD (0.3 g) and EDTA (0.2 g) dissolved in 10 mL of water; the raw material for the preparation of c-CDs was m-PD (0.2 g) and glucose (0.3 g) dissolved in 10 mL of water; the raw material for the preparation of d-CDs was pomegranate powder (0.5 g) and potassium thiocyanate (1 g) dissolved in 15 mL of water. Pomegranate powder is made from dried and ground pomegranate shells. The above solution is then transferred to a 50 mL stainless steel autoclave lined with PTFE and heated in an oven at 180 °C for 6 h (a-CDs) or 8 h (b, c, d-CDs).

The purification process of the CDs was carried out according to previous reports [31] from our group. In brief, at the end of the reaction, the above solution was sonicated for 10 min and then filtered through a 0.22 μm filter to remove large particles. The precipitate was then removed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm/min for 15 min. Finally the product was transferred to a dialysis bag with Mw = 500 Da to remove unreacted reagents and dialyzed for 72 h with hourly water changes. The four CDs powders were then freeze-dried and collected for subsequent use.

The preparation process of CDs@PAM was carried out according to our previous reports [31], in brief, 3 mg of CDs and 3 g of PAM in 20 mL of water were stirred well and transferred to an oven at 80 °C and heated for 10 h to obtain. Finally, the resulting lumpy solids were ground into fine particles.

Four CDs were characterized by HRTEM, XRD, XPS, Raman spectroscopy, and FTIR. The morphology of the four CDs were characterized by HRTEM. As shown in the electron micrographs in Fig. 2 a–d, the four CDs are spherical with a rather homogenous particle size distribution. The average particle sizes of the four CDs were 2.86, 3.18, 4.5 and 4.02 nm, respectively. The HRTEM images of the CDs showed that the four CDs had distinct lattice stripes with lattice spacing of 0.21, 0.21, 0.34 and 0.34 nm, respectively. From the XRD spectra (Fig. 2e) it can be observed that the peaks of the four CDs are located at 2θ = 25.3°, 21.8°, 24.5° and 25.6°, corresponding to the (100), (100), (002) and (002) lattice spacings of the graphite-like carbon group, respectively [32-36], and the XRD patterns of the four CDs showed similarly broad diffraction bands. The broad band reveals the amorphous nature and good crystallinity of the CDs.

The functional groups on the surface of the four CDs were identified using FTIR. As can be seen in Fig. 2f, a-CDs are rich in hydrophilic groups such as O–H [37] (3200 cm−1), C=C [24] (1570 cm−1), and C–O [38] (1180 cm−1). The broad absorption bands at 3280 cm−1 for the O–H/N–H [39] stretching vibration and at 1640 cm−1 and 1360 cm−1 for the C=C [40] and C–N [24] vibrational absorption bands, respectively, are functional groups that improved the hydrophilicity and stability of b-CDs in aqueous systems. In the FTIR curves of c-CDs, the peaks at about 3200 cm−1 are the characteristic absorption of O–H/N–H [41] stretching vibrations, a C–H [42] stretching vibration peak can be observed at 2900 cm−1, a C=C [43] vibrational absorption band at around 1620 cm−1, a C–N = [44] bending vibration peak at 1370 cm−1 and a C–O [45] vibrational absorption band at 1060 cm−1. For d-CDs, a stretching vibration peak of O–H/N–H [46] can be observed at 3360 cm−1 and stretching vibration peaks at 1570 cm−1, 1390 cm−1, 1050 cm−1 and 780 cm−1 are associated with stretching vibrations of C=C [47], C–N [48], C–O [48] and C–S [49], respectively. The presence of the above functional groups indicates that the CDs showed good dispersion in water.

The FTIR results were further confirmed by XPS. The chemical composition of the four CDs were obtained by the fitting of XPS peaks. According to the XPS spectra, the four CDs are primarily composed of C, N, O, and S. (Fig. 2g and Table S1). Among them, a-CDs are primarily composed of 70.84% C and 29.16% O. The C1s XPS spectra of a-CDs have four peaks at 283.7, 284.8, 286.3 and 288.9 eV which are attributed to C=C [50], C–C [47], C–O [47] and C=O [38], respectively. The O1s bands of C–O [51] and C=O [52] are composed of two peaks at 531.3 and 532.9 eV (Fig. S1). b-CDs are primarily composed of 56.3% C, 30.89% O and 12.81% N. The C1s XPS spectra of b-CDs have three peaks at 284.0, 285.3 and 287.6 eV which are attributed to C=C/C–C [53], C–N [54]and C=O [55], respectively. The N1s XPS spectrum contains three components: pyridine N [56] (398.5 eV), pyrrole N [53] (399.5 eV) and N–H [54] (401.2 eV). The O1s bands of C–O [55] and C=O [57] are composed of two peaks at 530.7 and 532.1 eV (Fig. S2).c-CDs are primarily composed of 76.15% C, 12.07% O and 11.78% N. The C1s XPS spectra of c-CDs have three peaks at 284.2, 285.0 and 286.0 eV which are attributed to C=C/C–C [53], C–N [54] and C–O [58], respectively. The N1s XPS spectrum contains two components: pyridine N [42] (398.9 eV) and pyrrole N [52] (399.7 eV). The O1s bands of C–O [59] and C=O [52] are composed of two peaks at 532.3 and 533.5 eV (Fig. S3).d-CDs are primarily composed of 74.25% C, 19.84% O, 2.72% N and 3.19% S. The C1s XPS spectra of d-CDs have three peaks at 284.7, 286.0 and 288.3 eV which are attributed to C=C/C–C [53], C–N/C–S [38] and C=O [52], respectively. The N1s XPS spectrum contains two components: pyridine N [58] (398.6 eV) and pyrrole N [48] (400.1 eV). The O1s bands of C–O [24] and C=O [60] are composed of two peaks at 531.2 and 532.7 eV. The S2p bands of C–S 2p3/2 [37] and C–S 2p1/2 [41] are composed of two peaks at 163.42 and 164.52 eV (Fig. S4). FTIR and XPS analyses showed that the surface of CDs is rich in oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups, which can effectively build intermolecular hydrogen bonds and prone to protonation and deprotonation [61].

In the Raman spectrum (Fig. 2h), the two peaks at 1321 cm−1 and 1573 cm−1 for a-CDs, 1365 cm−1 and 1577 cm−1 for b-CDs, 1382 cm−1 and 1596 cm−1 for c-CDs, and 1356 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 for d-CDs were the D and G bands, respectively. The D band was associated with the disordered sp3 carbon combined with the G-band represented the sp2 carbon of graphite. The intensity ratios (ID/IG) in the two bands were 0.54, 0.84, 0.61 and 0.51, respectively, demonstrating the presence of a lot of disordered carbon in addition to sp2 carbon and that the high degree of graphitization and crystallization impacts the wavelength variation of the CDs [62].

The optical properties of CDs were investigated. Fig. 3 a-d shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra, excitation spectra and fluorescence spectra of the four CDs in aqueous solution. The UV–Vis absorption spectra shows that a-CDs have two absorption peaks at 261 nm and 340 nm; b-CDs have two absorption peaks at 286 nm and 444 nm; c-CDs have two absorption peaks at 218 nm and 366 nm; and d-CDs have two absorption peaks at 254 nm and 352 nm. Where the absorption peaks at 261, 286, 218 and 254 nm can be attributed to π-π* transition of C=C bonds in the sp2 domain with the aromatic conjugate system. The second absorption peaks of the a-CDs and d-CDs near 340 and 352 nm may be due to electron transition associated with different functional groups (C=O, C=S, etc.) [63,64]. The b-CDs and c-CDs have an absorption peak at 444 and 366 nm, respectively, which can be attributed to the n-π* transition of the C=O/C=N functional group present in the CDs [48]. The presence of C=O/C=N groups can improve spin-orbit coupling, thus promoting inter-systemic crossover (ISC) and triplet exciton generation [65].

As shown in Fig. 3e–h, a-CDs, b-CDs and c-CDs exhibit excitation-independent fluorescence spectra in aqueous solution, with maximum emission wavelengths of 457, 539 and 532 nm at optimal excitation wavelengths of 366, 430 and 437 nm, respectively; while d-CDs exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra in aqueous solution, with maximum emission wavelengths of 454 nm at optimal excitation wavelengths of 350 nm. According to the optimal emission wavelengths of the four CDs in aqueous solution, it is shown that we have successfully prepared multicolor fluorescent CDs, providing a key precursor material for further preparation of multicolor CDs-based RTP materials. Notably, four CDs have no fluorescence in the solid state. In contrast to CDs dispersed in solution, CDs in the aggregated state were usually subject to excessive fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) due to π-π stacking interactions or distances between adjacent CDs less than R0, which in turn led to aggregation caused quenching [66]. Using quinine sulfate as reference, the fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of the four CDs are measured to be 13.5%, 8.7%, 4% and 17.6%, respectively. According to the formula of fluorescence lifetime reported in literature [25], the average fluorescence lifetimes of four CDs in aqueous solution were calculated to be 3.48, 4.86, 1.30 and 4.05 ns, respectively (Fig. S5).

Typically, RTP can be achieved by establishing a rigid environment to stabilize the excited triplet state and suppress non-radiative transition [67]. More specifically, by introducing suitable matrix to constrain the vibrations and rotations of the molecule, forming intermolecular hydrogen bonds [54] or covalent bonds between CDs and matrix [68]. In order to avoid the aggregation-caused quenching of CDs and to produce RTP, the present work combined CDs with PAM to obtain RTP materials. The higher hydrogen bonding affinity of the PAM surface results in enhanced optical properties of CDs@PAM.

As can be seen in Fig. 4 a–d, when each of the four CDs were complexed with PAM, the fluorescence emission wavelengths were 444, 484, 510 and 529 nm, respectively (CDs@PAM). It shows that four CDs@PAM composites prepared exhibit multi-color fluorescence. FTIR plots of CDs@PAM (Fig. 4e) show that the absorption at 3400 cm−1 is attributed to O–H stretching vibrations, the absorption vibration peak at 1700 cm−1 is C=O and the absorption at 1560 cm−1 is attributed to N–H stretching vibrations. This demonstrates that the composites have a large number of N–H and C=O groups, thus indicating that four CDs were involved in the assembly process with the PAM [25].

Impressively, the phosphorescence of solid-state CDs@PAM composites can be activated by visible light. The visible light source (Fig. S6) uses Alonefire brand WRGB LED (built-in 18650 lithium battery with a range of about 100 m) to excite the CDs@PAM, allowing full-color RTP in blue, green, yellow and red to be easily obtained (SⅢ. image). However, there were no RTP emissions observed when CDs were dispersed in water and other polymeric matrices such as starch, so PAM plays an important role in RTP. Fig. 4f shows that the phosphorescence emission wavelengths of the four CDs@PAM composites are located at 450, 504, 566 and 610 nm, respectively, under WLED excitation. The four phosphorescence spectra correspond to commission international eclairage (CIE) coordinates (Fig. 4g) of (0.20, 0.23), (0.26, 0.53), (0.53, 0.46) and (0.63, 0.36) respectively, demonstrating these materials' ability to emit multi-color phosphorescence.

Compared to fluorescence, the T1 state of phosphorescent radiation cannot be generated from direct absorbing photons in the S0 state and must undergo a forbidden transition process from S1 to T1. In addition, the triplet state was sensitive to factors such as water, oxygen and temperature, resulting in the phosphorescence non-radiative attenuation. On this basis, to summarize the luminescence mechanism of the CDs@PAM materials. The RTP of CDs@PAM is mainly derived from the C=O bonds of CDs. The n-π* electron transition of the C=O bonds facilitates the intersystem crossover process, which is necessary for phosphorescence generation. Here, the role of the PAM was to create a rigid protective environment (hydrogen bonding network structure [25]) that suppressed the vibrations, rotations and collisions of the luminescent clusters (C=O), thus avoiding the rapid decay of the phosphorescence. The introduction of the PAM matrix brings three advantages. Firstly, the doping of N elements with lone pairs of electrons promotes the n-π* transition and facilitates the spin-orbit coupling effect [69,70]. Secondly, the addition of the amide carbonyl group (C=N) generates a large number of new energy levels, resulting in a sharp reduction in the energy difference (ΔEST) between the lowest single state and the lowest triplet state, which is calculated from equation (1) [71] to be about 0.04–0.31 eV (Table 1 , Fig. 5 a left).
(1)
Δ
E ST
( eV )
=
1240
λ F
( nm )
−
1240
λ P
( nm )
where, λF is the peak fluorescence emission wavelength and λP is the peak phosphorescence emission wavelength.

A smaller ΔEST can facilitate the jump from the singly excited state to the triple excited state by forbidden resistance, which is more beneficial for room temperature phosphorescence. Thirdly, the hydrogen bonds formed between PAM matrix and CDs further increase the rigidity of the environment (Fig. 5a right) and largely suppress the non-radiative transition, favoring the production of phosphorescence in these materials [72]. Finally, due to the unique visible light-excited multi-color phosphorescent properties, CDs@PAM can be used in certain applications. As a preliminary example, their potential for anti-counterfeiting is illustrated. The University of Science and Technology of Liaoning's badge is covered on a platform of CDs@PAM, showing full-color phosphorescent emission when the WLED is switched off (Fig. 5b).

In summary, we report four color-adjustable CDs@PAM phosphorescent composites excited by visible light. These materials have two advantages. Firstly, visible light can be used more safely than UV light, which makes it easy to use in practical applications. Secondly, the color can be adjusted to ensure that the material has a strong resistance to background interference. Further, we investigated the optical properties and the phosphorescence mechanisms of CDs@PAM materials. The phosphorescence of the CDs@PAM composites is mainly derived from the electron transition at n-π* of the C=O bond of the CDs, which would facilitate the crossover between the systems. On the other hand, the hydrogen bonding environment provided by the PAM matrix and the introduction of the amide group largely suppress the non-radiative transition of the triplet exciton. Given the advantages of visible light excitation with adjustable color, we give an example of their potential application in anti-counterfeiting.

Huiyong Wang and Bowen Shi: Experimental design, synthesis and characterization; Yang Shu and Guochao Nie: supervision, writing and funding; Hongmei Yu, Shaoyan Wang and Wei Chen: Project leading, Writing, validation, supervision, funding and visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have not conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial supports from the XingLiao Talent Project Grants (No. XLYC1902076) and the Commercialization Gap Fund (CGF) from UT Arlington are highly appreciated.
